title: Leie

infobox.value: (French pronunciation: [[lis]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q150_(fra)-J%C3%A9r%C3%A9my-G%C3%BCnther-Heinz_J%C3%A4hnick-lis.wav "File:LL-Q150 (fra)-Jérémy-Günther-Heinz Jähnick-lis.wav")) or **Leie** (Dutch pronunciation: [[ˈlɛiə]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Dutch "Help:IPA/Dutch") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Nl-Leie.ogg "File:Nl-Leie.ogg")) is a [river](/wiki/River "River") in [France](/wiki/France "France") and [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), and a left-bank tributary of the [Scheldt](/wiki/Scheldt "Scheldt"). Its source is in [Pas-de-Calais](/wiki/Pas-de-Calais "Pas-de-Calais"), France, and it flows into the river [Scheldt](/wiki/Scheldt "Scheldt"), with the confluence in the city of [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent"), Belgium. Its total length is 202 kilometres (126 mi).

[Sandre](/wiki/Service_d%27administration_nationale_des_donn%C3%A9es_et_r%C3%A9f%C3%A9rentiels_sur_l%27eau "Service d'administration nationale des données et référentiels sur l'eau"). ["Fiche cours d'eau - La Lys (E3--0120)"](https://www.sandre.eaufrance.fr/geo/CoursEau_Carthage2017/E3--0120).

["Terrible Disaster"](https://www.newspapers.com/clip/12387804/terrible_disaster/). Green Bay, Wisconsin: Green Bay Semi-Weekly Gazette. 27 December 1899. p. 1. Retrieved 15 July 2017 – via newspapers.com.

Edwards-May, David (2010). *Inland Waterways of Francea*. St Ives, Cambs., UK: Imray Ltd. pp. 127–129. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-1-846230-14-1](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-1-846230-14-1 "Special:BookSources/978-1-846230-14-1").

[Coordinates](/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_system "Geographic coordinate system"): [51°3′18″N 3°44′3″E﻿ / ﻿51.05500°N 3.73417°E﻿ / 51.05500; 3.73417](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Lys_(river)&params=51_3_18_N_3_44_3_E_type:river)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Leie](/w/index.php?title=Leie&redirect=no "Leie"))

River in France and Belgium

This article is about the river in France and Belgium. For the tributary of the Dora Baltea in Italy, see [Lys (Dora Baltea)](/wiki/Lys_(Dora_Baltea) "Lys (Dora Baltea)").

"Leie" redirects here. For Leie, Estonia, see [Kolga-Jaani Parish](/wiki/Kolga-Jaani_Parish "Kolga-Jaani Parish").

The **Lys** (French pronunciation: [[lis]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q150_(fra)-J%C3%A9r%C3%A9my-G%C3%BCnther-Heinz_J%C3%A4hnick-lis.wav "File:LL-Q150 (fra)-Jérémy-Günther-Heinz Jähnick-lis.wav")) or **Leie** (Dutch pronunciation: [[ˈlɛiə]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Dutch "Help:IPA/Dutch") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Nl-Leie.ogg "File:Nl-Leie.ogg")) is a [river](/wiki/River "River") in [France](/wiki/France "France") and [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), and a left-bank tributary of the [Scheldt](/wiki/Scheldt "Scheldt"). Its source is in [Pas-de-Calais](/wiki/Pas-de-Calais "Pas-de-Calais"), France, and it flows into the river [Scheldt](/wiki/Scheldt "Scheldt"), with the confluence in the city of [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent"), Belgium. Its total length is 202 kilometres (126 mi).

Historically a very polluted river from the high population density and industrialisation in both Northern France and Belgium, it has seen substantial improvements in recent years, partly due to the decline of the principal industry, the spinning and weaving of [flax](/wiki/Flax "Flax"). The region of the Leie (between Deinze and Ghent) was known as a favourite place for numerous painters in the first half of the 20th century.

The source of the Lys is in a village, [Lisbourg](/wiki/Lisbourg "Lisbourg"), east of [Fruges](/wiki/Fruges "Fruges"), in the [Pas-de-Calais](/wiki/Pas-de-Calais "Pas-de-Calais") department of France. It flows generally northeast through the following [departments of France](/wiki/Departments_of_France "Departments of France"), [provinces of Belgium](/wiki/Provinces_of_Belgium "Provinces of Belgium") and towns and municipalities:

* [Pas-de-Calais](/wiki/Pas-de-Calais "Pas-de-Calais") (F): [Thérouanne](/wiki/Th%C3%A9rouanne "Thérouanne"), [Aire-sur-la-Lys](/wiki/Aire-sur-la-Lys "Aire-sur-la-Lys")
* [Nord](/wiki/Nord_(d%C3%A9partement) "Nord (département)") (F): [Merville](/wiki/Merville,_Nord "Merville, Nord"), [Armentières](/wiki/Armenti%C3%A8res "Armentières"), [Halluin](/wiki/Halluin "Halluin")
* [Hainaut](/wiki/Hainaut_(province) "Hainaut (province)") (B): [Comines-Warneton](/wiki/Comines-Warneton "Comines-Warneton")
* [West Flanders](/wiki/West_Flanders "West Flanders") (B): [Menen](/wiki/Menen "Menen"), [Wevelgem](/wiki/Wevelgem "Wevelgem"), [Kortrijk](/wiki/Kortrijk "Kortrijk"), [Waregem](/wiki/Waregem "Waregem"), [Wervik](/wiki/Wervik "Wervik")
* [East Flanders](/wiki/East_Flanders "East Flanders") (B): [Zulte](/wiki/Zulte "Zulte"), [Deinze](/wiki/Deinze "Deinze"), [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent")

[![Lys in Ghent](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Blick_%C3%BCber_die_Leie_in_Gent_%28Belgien%29.jpg/250px-Blick_%C3%BCber_die_Leie_in_Gent_%28Belgien%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Blick_%C3%BCber_die_Leie_in_Gent_(Belgien).jpg)

The river in [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Buildings_along_the_Leie%2C_South_of_the_Zuivelbrug%2C_Ghent%2C_Belgium.jpg/250px-Buildings_along_the_Leie%2C_South_of_the_Zuivelbrug%2C_Ghent%2C_Belgium.jpg)](/wiki/File:Buildings_along_the_Leie,_South_of_the_Zuivelbrug,_Ghent,_Belgium.jpg)

Buildings south along the Leie in [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent")

The main tributaries of the Leie are, from source to mouth: [Laquette](/wiki/Laquette "Laquette"), [Clarence](/wiki/Clarence_(river) "Clarence (river)"), [Lawe](/wiki/Lawe "Lawe"), [Deûle](/wiki/De%C3%BBle "Deûle"), [Gaverbeek](/w/index.php?title=Gaverbeek&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gaverbeek (page does not exist)") [[nl](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gaverbeek "nl:Gaverbeek")], [Heulebeek](/w/index.php?title=Heulebeek&action=edit&redlink=1 "Heulebeek (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heule_(ruisseau) "fr:Heule (ruisseau)"); [nl](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heulebeek "nl:Heulebeek"); [vls](https://vls.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eulebeke "vls:Eulebeke")], and [Mandel](/wiki/Mandel_(river) "Mandel (river)").[[1]](#cite_note-sandre-1)

The river was the location of three battles between the Allies and the German Army during the two world wars. During the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War") in 1918 the location was the scene of the [First Battle of the Lys](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Lys_(1918) "Battle of the Lys (1918)"), which was part of the [German Spring Offensive](/wiki/German_spring_offensive "German spring offensive") and later that year of the Second Battle of the Lys, which was part of the Allies' [Hundred Days Offensive](/wiki/Hundred_Days_Offensive "Hundred Days Offensive"). During the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), the [Battle of the Lys (1940)](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Lys_(1940) "Battle of the Lys (1940)") was part of the Nazi German Wehrmacht's Blitzkrieg offensive towards the English Channel.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lys_(river)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The Leie/Lys was a commercial navigation from the Middle Ages, but it was the river's devastating floods rather than navigation improvements which justified major works and meander cut-offs started around 1670. The 9 meter difference in elevation between Aire-sur-la-Lys and the border was gradually overcome by six locks and weirs, completed in 1780. The river carried a heavy traffic in grain and linen through to Ghent and Antwerp. The navigation was leased out to a company around 1825, and the locks upgraded to 5.20 m wide, for a draught of 1.60 m. In December 1899, more than 40 schoolchildren skating on the frozen river at [Frelinghien](/wiki/Frelinghien "Frelinghien") fell through the ice and drowned.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The river was given its present depth by the [Freycinet programme](/wiki/Freycinet_gauge "Freycinet gauge"). The section below Armentières was enlarged to class III from 1930, and the border section was improved to class Va starting from 1960. This section is part of the EU's priority project 30 for the Seine-Escaut waterway. The upstream section, by contrast, is used almost exclusively by recreational craft, and is at present heavily silted.[[3]](#cite_note-3)
